{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",

runContext.design_guide:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — All Surfaces Version: 1.1 (React Router + article system adaptation) Scope: Branding, tokens, theming, component usage + defaults, accessibility, and article-content implementation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI/content that is visually consistent, inclusive, and build-safe in this repository without guessing. Applies to: `app/articles/content/**`, shared article components in `app/components/articles/**`, and related UI surfaces. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette (tokens) MLAI uses dark neutrals + vivid accents (teal/orange/purple/yellow). In this codebase, both CSS variables and hardcoded accent hexes are used. | Token | Hex | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | `#00E6C2` | Brand accent surface / bullets / emphasis | | `--brand-ink` | `#007A66` | Brand text/link colour on light surfaces | | `--accent` | `#111827` | Primary dark text | | `--soft` | `#E0FFFA` | Soft brand-tinted backgrounds | | `--highlight` | `#F59E0B` | Highlight accent (limited emphasis) | | Accent Orange | `#ff3d00` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Purple | `#4b1bd1` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Yellow | `#ffe900` | MLAI high-contrast card/callout surface | #### Guardrails (WCAG AA) - On light backgrounds, use `text-gray-900`/`text-gray-800`/`text-[--brand-ink]`. - Avoid white text on bright teal unless contrast is verified. - On orange/purple surfaces, use `text-white`. - On yellow surfaces, use `text-black`. - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline`. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape - Tailwind utilities are the default styling system. - Typography plugin is enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`). - Rounded styles in active use: - `rounded-lg`, `rounded-2xl`, `rounded-3xl`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-[32px]` - Prefer generous spacing in article modules (`my-8`, `my-12`, `p-6`, `p-8`). --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus - Keep transitions subtle (`transition-colors`, `duration-200`). - Focus styles should remain visible (`focus:ring-*`, `focus-visible:ring-*`). - Do not remove focus outlines. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity - AU English. - Practical, clear, no hype/guarantees. - No unverifiable claims of outcomes. - Keep CTAs generic (e.g. **“Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”**). - Do not add medical disclaimers unless explicitly requested in source template/content requirements. --- ## 2) Theming & tokens for code ### 2.1 Framework and styling reality (authoritative) - Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.
runContext.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

tech_stack.framework_prompt_context: DETECTED FRAMEWORK: React Router RENDERING LIBRARY: react 19.2.1 ROUTING: react-router-7 (import from 'react-router', links use 'to' prop) STYLING: tailwind LANGUAGE: TypeScript COMPONENT FILE EXTENSION: .tsx BUILD TOOL: vite CRITICAL RULES FOR CODE GENERATION: - Do NOT use next/link, next/image, next/dynamic — these are Next.js-only packages - Do NOT use 'use client' or 'use server' directives — these are Next.js App Router only - Do NOT use getStaticProps, getServerSideProps, generateStaticParams — Next.js only - Do NOT use export const metadata — Next.js App Router only - Do NOT use next/font — Next.js only - Do NOT use Vue SFC (.vue files), Composition API, or Options API — not a Vue project - Do NOT use Svelte syntax (.svelte files), $: reactivity, or Svelte stores USE THESE PATTERNS INSTEAD: - For links: import from 'react-router' and use the 'to' prop - For images: use standard HTML tags - For styling: use plain CSS or the project's detected styling approach

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI -- One-Shot Article Content Generation Spec (React Router TSX) -- v2026-04-26 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent (as a "system" / "developer" prompt) so the agent can generate brand-consistent MLAI article content modules in one shot for this repository. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Content should be practical, inclusive, and factual. Avoid hype and guarantees. Output format: TypeScript React component file (`.tsx`) for article body content. Framework: React Router 7 + React 19 + Tailwind. Import style: alias-first with `~/...` paths. --- ## 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given article inputs (slug/category/topic/body sections/FAQ/references/CTA text), generate a production-ready article content TSX file that compiles **without manual edits**. Framework constraints (required): - Use `react-router` APIs (e.g., `Link` with `to` prop). - Do **not** use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `use client`, metadata exports, etc.). - Do **not** use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use Tailwind utility classes consistent with this repo. Architecture constraints (required): - **NO `ArticleLayout` in content files**. Route handler wraps content already. - Content component must return `<>...` or `
...
`. - If using wrapper div, use `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Use design system components when available: - Persona cards → `AudienceGrid` (`variant`: `orange | purple | yellow` only) - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` (`variant`: `purple | orange` only) - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` (no raw `` blocks for article body sections) - Never use `placehold.co` URLs. Use `` or empty string placeholders. - Ensure `seo_config` path matches registry slug path exactly. Deliverable: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` (If requested, also provide companion updates for registry/SEO entries with matching slug/path.) --- ## 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: - `title`: string - `slug`: string - `category`: string (folder under `app/articles/content/`) - `summary`: string - `sections`: ordered list of body sections - `audienceCards` (optional): for "Who is this for?" - `quoteBlocks` (optional) - `images` (optional) - `faqItems` (optional) - `references` (optional) - `cta` (optional, generic wording only) Use placeholders exactly as provided (e.g., `{{TOPIC}}`). --- ## 2) Framework, file, and styling configuration - Language: TypeScript (`.tsx`) - Component pattern: `export default function ...() { ... }` - Routing library: `react-router` - Styling: Tailwind classes already used in repo - Typography plugin enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`) - Internal imports should prefer `~/...` aliases Common class patterns in this codebase: - Layout/spacing: `my-8`, `my-12`, `mt-8`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-3xl`, `p-6`, `sm:p-8` - Text colors: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600`, `text-white` - Borders/surfaces: `border border-gray-300`, `border-gray-400`, `bg-transparent` - Prose-safe wrappers for custom UI blocks: `not-prose` --- ## 3) Import rules (use real project paths) Use only imports that exist in this codebase. Typical imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Do not invent components/props. --- ## 4) Article content structure (no duplicate page header) Important: layout/hero/breadcrumb/title are handled upstream. So inside content file: - Start directly with body content (`h2`, `p`, lists, blocks). - Do **not** render duplicate title/breadcrumb/author header. - Keep heading hierarchy valid (`h2` for top-level sections, `h3` nested). Recommended section flow: 1. Intro paragraph(s) 2. Main sections (`h2`) 3. Optional persona block (`AudienceGrid`) 4. Optional quote block (`QuoteBlock`) 5. Optional image block (`ArticleImageBlock`) 6. FAQ (`ArticleFAQ`) 7. Generic CTA 8. Footer nav (`ArticleFooterNav`) --- ## 5) Design system component usage (strict) ### 5.1 Audience cards Use `AudienceGrid` for "Who is this for?" sections: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants only: `orange | purple | yellow`. ### 5.2 Quotes/callouts Use `QuoteBlock` (not ad-hoc bordered divs): ```tsx Keep your first AI implementation narrow, measurable, and reviewable. ``` Allowed variants only: `purple | orange`. ### 5.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`: ```tsx ``` No placeholder services (e.g., no `placehold.co`). --- ## 6) Links, references, and navigation - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` with `to`. - External links: `...`. - Use authoritative references when included. - Footer navigation can use: ```tsx ``` --- ## 7) CTA rules - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - ✅ Example: `Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}` - ❌ Do not use product-specific or service-specific names unless explicitly provided. Example with existing component: ```tsx ``` --- ## 8) Content quality and tone rules - Australian context where relevant. - Practical, factual, no guarantees. - No medical/legal-style warning blocks unless explicitly requested. - No service chips/tags sections. - Keep paragraphs concise and scannable. - Prefer concrete steps and examples. --- ## 9) Build-safety rules (one-shot accuracy) - File must compile with strict TypeScript. - Use valid, existing imports only. - Do not add unsupported props to components. - Keep variant strings within known unions. - Avoid fragile breadcrumb/icon patterns in content files. - Keep slug/path naming consistent across file path and SEO/registry references. --- ## 10) Brand QA checklist (must pass) - Uses repository design components for cards/callouts/images. - No duplicate hero/title section in content body. - Wrapper uses `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Tailwind classes align with existing style language. - CTA text remains generic with placeholders preserved. - No invented components. --- ## 11) Technical QA checklist (must pass) - React Router imports only (`react-router`). - No Next.js/Vue/Svelte APIs. - `.tsx` syntax valid. - All JSX tags closed and typed. - Internal links use `to`, external links use `href`. - No broken alias paths (`~/...`). --- ## 12) Critical do/don't summary (hard guardrails) DO - Generate `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx`. - Export a default function component. - Use `AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock` where applicable. - Use only allowed variants. - Preserve placeholders like `{{TOPIC}}`. - Keep content body-only (no layout wrapper). NEVER - Import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Use `bg-white` wrapper backgrounds. - Use unsupported variants (`blue`, `brand` for these DS blocks). - Use `placehold.co`. - Invent imports, components, or props. - Add duplicate page title/breadcrumb header inside content. --- ## 13) Suggested content primitives (repo-aligned) - Section heading: `

...

` - Body copy: `

...

` - Persona block: `AudienceGrid` - Quote/callout: `QuoteBlock` - Image: `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ: `ArticleFAQ` with `ArticleFAQItem[]` - CTA: `ArticleCompanyCTA` - Footer nav: `ArticleFooterNav` --- ## 14) Example scaffold (copy/paste starter) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the first step?', answer: 'Define one clear outcome for {{TOPIC}}.' }, ] export default function ArticleContent() { return (

This guide explains practical ways to approach {{TOPIC}}.

Who is this for?

Key insight

Start narrow, track results, and iterate.
) } ``` --- ## 15) How to use this prompt 1. Parse inputs (`{{TOPIC}}`, slug, category, sections, FAQ, CTA). 2. Generate a single `.tsx` article content module at: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` 3. Use existing repository components and exact import paths. 4. Keep output body-only (no `ArticleLayout` / duplicate hero). 5. Validate TypeScript + React Router compatibility. 6. Ensure slug/path consistency for any SEO/registry references requested. End of master spec (React Router TSX, MLAI codebase-aligned). ```
org_config_snapshot.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

tech_stack.framework_prompt_context: DETECTED FRAMEWORK: React Router RENDERING LIBRARY: react 19.2.1 ROUTING: react-router-7 (import from 'react-router', links use 'to' prop) STYLING: tailwind LANGUAGE: TypeScript COMPONENT FILE EXTENSION: .tsx BUILD TOOL: vite CRITICAL RULES FOR CODE GENERATION: - Do NOT use next/link, next/image, next/dynamic — these are Next.js-only packages - Do NOT use 'use client' or 'use server' directives — these are Next.js App Router only - Do NOT use getStaticProps, getServerSideProps, generateStaticParams — Next.js only - Do NOT use export const metadata — Next.js App Router only - Do NOT use next/font — Next.js only - Do NOT use Vue SFC (.vue files), Composition API, or Options API — not a Vue project - Do NOT use Svelte syntax (.svelte files), $: reactivity, or Svelte stores USE THESE PATTERNS INSTEAD: - For links: import from 'react-router' and use the 'to' prop - For images: use standard HTML tags - For styling: use plain CSS or the project's detected styling approach

pillars.name: AI Community Events and Meetups in Australia
pillars.description: This pillar focuses on discovering, choosing, and getting value from AI meetups, workshops, lightning talks, and community events across Australia. It directly supports MLAI's events programming for people who want practical learning, relevant peers, and a clearer entry point into the local AI community.
pillars.content_angle: A practical, community-first guide to finding real AI connections and hands-on learning through Australian events rather than generic networking.

pillars.name: AI Hackathons and Real-World Challenge Programs
pillars.description: This pillar covers hackathons, challenge-based programs, team formation, prototyping, and applied AI problem-solving. It aligns closely with MLAI's hackathons and event programs, helping builders, students, founders, and domain experts turn ideas into tangible projects.
pillars.content_angle: Show how hackathons can be practical pathways to collaboration, prototypes, and startup momentum, not just short-term coding competitions.

pillars.name: Practical AI Learning and Getting Started
pillars.description: This pillar helps beginners and early-stage practitioners learn AI and machine learning through accessible, community-based pathways. It supports MLAI's educational content, workshops, and events by answering common learning questions with practical next steps and applied examples.
pillars.content_angle: Lower the barrier to entry by pairing plain-English education with hands-on community participation instead of theory-heavy content alone.

pillars.name: AI Founder Support, Pitching and Investor Readiness
pillars.description: This pillar addresses how early-stage AI founders can build momentum, sharpen their pitch, create better updates, and start investor relationship-building earlier. It maps to MLAI's founder-oriented tools, practical guides, and community programs that help entrepreneurs move from idea to credible signal.
pillars.content_angle: Focus on founder communication and investor readiness as practical habits supported by community, feedback, and early visibility.
seed_keywords: how to pitch an ai startup

pillars.name: Cross-Disciplinary AI Collaboration
pillars.description: This pillar explores how technical builders, clinicians, researchers, and other domain experts can work together on real-world AI challenges. It reflects MLAI's community and hackathon model, where useful outcomes depend on better collaboration between technical and non-technical participants.
pillars.content_angle: Bridge the gap between builders and domain experts with practical guidance for team formation, problem definition, and applied outcomes.
seed_keywords: technical and non technical collaboration ai

pillars.name: Community Participation and Contributor Recognition
pillars.description: This pillar explains how members can contribute meaningfully inside a community, build credibility, and earn recognition through participation. It directly connects to MLAI's Slack community and Roo Points system, giving contributors a clearer path to involvement, visibility, and useful perks.
pillars.content_angle: Position contribution as a practical way to build relationships and credibility in an AI community, without framing it as a guaranteed pathway to jobs or compensation.
seed_keywords: how to contribute to a tech community

pillar_strategy.rationale: These pillars were chosen because they map directly to MLAI's strongest audience needs and core offerings: events, hackathons, practical learning, founder support, cross-disciplinary collaboration, and community participation. Together they cover the main discovery journeys of learners, builders, founders, contributors, and domain experts while staying grounded in MLAI's role as a practical, community-first home base for applied AI in Australia.

spec.title: A practical guide to startup funding in Australia
spec.meta_description: Startup funding Australia options for early founders, including grants, loans, angel investment, crowdfunding and readiness steps.

summary_items.label: How do I get funding for my startup?
summary_items.description: Choose a funding path that fits your stage, such as a grant, loan, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding or bootstrapping. Then prepare evidence and apply through the relevant route.

summary_items.label: How do I get funding for my startup idea?
summary_items.description: At idea stage, focus first on customer discovery, validation evidence and a clear next milestone. Small grants, bootstrapping or targeted support may fit better than a broad raise.

summary_items.label: How do you get funding for a start-up?
summary_items.description: Start by matching the funding source to the business goal, traction and repayment or growth story. Funders usually need clear evidence of the problem, model, team and use of funds.

summary_question_candidates.question: How do I get funding for my startup?
How do I get funding for my startup idea?
How do you get funding for a start-up?
What are the five types of startups?
What business can I start with $100K in Australia?
What is the business start up grant in Australia?
How much money will the government give me for starting a business?

faq_items.question: What startup funding options are available in Australia?
faq_items.answer: Australian startups can consider government grants and programs, loans, rebates, subsidies, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and bootstrapping. The right option depends on stage, eligibility, evidence and the purpose of the funding.

faq_items.question: Is there one business start up grant in Australia?
faq_items.answer: No. Startup support is not a single national grant. It is a changing mix of federal, state, territory and industry-specific programs, so founders should check current grant finder tools and relevant state support pages.

faq_items.question: How much money will the government give me for starting a business?
faq_items.answer: There is no universal amount. Government support varies by program, location, eligibility, funding purpose and round timing, and may include grants, loans, rebates or subsidies.

faq_items.question: When should a startup seek angel investment or venture capital?
faq_items.answer: Angel investment or venture capital is usually more suitable when a startup can show a credible growth story, early traction, a clear business model and a strong use of funds. Founders should also be ready for dilution, scrutiny and governance expectations.

faq_items.question: What should founders prepare before applying for funding?
faq_items.answer: Founders should be able to explain the customer problem, solution, market, traction, team capability, business model, financial logic and intended use of funds. Grant applications should also answer the specific eligibility and assessment criteria.

sections.heading: Why startup funding in Australia needs a staged plan
paragraphs: Startup funding in Australia is not one single pathway. Founders can look at government grants and support programs, loans, rebates, subsidies, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and other forms of capital. The useful question is not only “where can we get money?” It is “which funding source fits our stage, evidence and goals?”

A grant or support program may help with a specific project, while investor capital usually needs a stronger story about growth, traction and the business model. Funding also sits inside a wider startup plan: customers, collaborators, talent, advisors and community support all affect whether capital can turn into real progress.

sections.heading: Start with the Australian funding landscape
paragraphs: Startup funding in Australia is not one single market. It includes national, state and territory programs, plus local startup ecosystems that help founders find support, introductions and momentum. The NSW Small Business Commissioner notes that grants, loans, rebates and subsidies are available across a range of industries, business types and purposes, including startups.

The scale of that support also means founders need to filter carefully. The same NSW source says the Federal Government grant finder listed 537 different grants and programs for businesses, with NSW also offering its own grant finder. For a founder, this is useful but noisy. A better first step is to narrow by location, sector, business stage and the purpose of the funding.

State and city ecosystems matter because funding often comes through more than a form on a website. South Australia shows how local ecosystems can build startup capacity over time. The Department of State Development says South Australia’s startup ecosystem has grown significantly over the last decade, and cites Startup Genome 2024 ranking Adelaide as a Top 5 city in Oceania for overall sector performance.

For Australian AI and machine learning founders, the practical lesson is to map both money and ecosystem access. Look at grants and programs, but also look at the networks around them: events, innovation hubs, startup support programs and local industry communities. These can help founders understand what support fits their stage before they spend time on applications or investor conversations.

callout.title: Funding starts with fit
callout.body: Shortlist opportunities by state, sector, business stage and funding purpose before you spend time on applications or investor outreach.

sections.heading: Match the funding option to your stage
paragraphs: The best choice for startup funding in Australia depends on what you are trying to prove next. A grant, loan, angel cheque or crowdfunding campaign can all be useful in the right context.

Government-backed grants can help with eligible business activity, innovation projects, training, hiring or other defined purposes. They are worth checking at federal, state and territory level, but founders should expect competition and prepare a strong application. Conventional finance and business loans are different. They are usually better suited to businesses with clearer revenue, assets or repayment capacity, rather than a very early idea with uncertain cash flow.

Equity funding is usually a growth decision, not just a cash decision. Angel investors and venture capital can bring capital, networks and experience, but they also come with investor scrutiny, dilution and governance expectations. Equity crowdfunding can be another path for some startups, especially where the product and audience support a public campaign, but it needs to fit the company’s compliance needs and ability to run the campaign well.

subsections.heading: A simple stage-by-stage comparison
paragraphs: At the idea validation stage, founders should be careful about chasing capital that requires a polished growth story before the basics are clear. Small grants, bootstrapping and customer discovery work may be more realistic than loans or equity rounds. The aim is to test whether the problem, user and early offer are strong enough to justify more formal funding.

At the early traction stage, the funding question becomes more specific. If you have users, early revenue or a clearer product plan, grants may support defined projects and angel investors may be more willing to look at the opportunity. At scale preparation, founders need a stronger business foundation, a clear capital strategy and investor-ready materials before approaching larger equity investors or more structured finance options.

sections.heading: Build the evidence funders expect to see
paragraphs: Before you chase startup funding in Australia, make the business easier to assess. A strong foundation means you can explain the customer problem, the solution, the business model and the growth goal in plain terms. This matters across capital types. Investor pathways, grants, loans and other funding options all become harder when the founder cannot show why the business needs money now and how that money supports growth.

For investor funding, prepare a clear pitch deck that covers the problem, solution, market, traction, team, business model and intended use of funds. For grants, treat the application as a direct response to the program, not a generic company profile. Read the eligibility criteria closely, match your answers to the purpose of the grant, and show why your business fits the funding opportunity. Grant programs can be competitive, so clarity and relevance matter.
bullets: Define the funding goal: decide what the money will help you do and which funding path fits that goal.
Tailor the pitch or application: adjust the deck or grant response to the funder, the criteria and the stage of your startup.

sections.heading: Answer the funding questions before you apply
paragraphs: Before you apply for startup funding in Australia, be clear on what kind of funding you are seeking and why it fits your stage. Australian founders can look at several paths, including bank finance, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and government-backed grants. The right choice depends on the business goal, the evidence you already have and the type of capital you can realistically use well.

If you are still at idea stage, the first funding question is not usually “how much can I raise?” It is “what evidence will make this idea worth funding?” Customer discovery, early validation and a focused next milestone can make a small funding request stronger than a broad raise with little proof. Investors and grant assessors both need a reason to believe the business can turn funding into progress.

Government support is not one fixed startup grant or one standard amount. Federal, state and territory programs change over time, and support can include grants, loans, rebates and subsidies for different industries, business types and purposes. Founders should check current grant finder tools and the relevant state or territory support pages before planning around any specific program.

The same logic applies if you have a defined budget, such as $100K, and are deciding what business to start. The better question is which model can turn that money into validated demand, measurable traction and a fundable next step. A capital plan should show how the money moves the startup from its current stage to the next credible milestone, not just how much cash the founder hopes to receive.

sections.heading: Choose your next funding move
paragraphs: The best next step is not to chase every startup funding Australia option at once. Shortlist two or three routes that fit your current stage, such as grants, angel investment, venture capital, bank finance, equity crowdfunding or bootstrapping. Then check the timing, eligibility and effort required before you commit time to an application or raise.

Use the next week to tighten the evidence behind your funding story. Make sure you can explain the customer problem, your early traction, the team’s capability, the financial logic and how the funds will be used. Grants are competitive, so a stronger application matters. Investors also expect a clear business foundation and a funding strategy that matches your goals.

Australian startup ecosystems are built around events, networks, startup supports and founder communities. For AI and machine learning founders, MLAI can help turn funding preparation into practical momentum through learning, founder tools and conversations with other Australian builders.

cta.title: Build your funding plan with the AI founder community
cta.body: Use MLAI to connect funding preparation with practical learning, founder tools and conversations with other Australian AI and machine learning builders.

notes: Grounded sections meet the planned featured article structure with six sections, three prose sections, a subsection, summary highlights, FAQs, CTA support and required shell components. Keep the article focused on matching funding to stage rather than promising access to capital.

Selected summary questions: How do I get funding for my startup? (paa, vol=0, ai=21); How do I get funding for my startup idea? (paa, vol=0, ai=1); How do you get funding for a start-up? (paa, vol=0, ai=14)

import { useEffect } from 'react'
import type { ReactNode } from 'react'
import { Home } from 'lucide-react'
import { RocketLaunchIcon, AcademicCapIcon, UsersIcon } from '@heroicons/react/24/outline'
import { DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY, getAuthorProfile, DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL } from '../../authors'
import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ'
import ArticleCompanyCTA from '../../../components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA'
import AuthorBio from '../../../components/AuthorBio'
import { ArticleHeroHeader } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleHeroHeader'
import { ArticleImageBlock } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleImageBlock'
import { ArticleFooterNav } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFooterNav'
import { QuoteBlock } from '../../../components/articles/QuoteBlock'
import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder'
import { AudienceGrid } from '../../../components/articles/AudienceGrid'
import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA'
import { ArticleStepList } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleStepList'
import { ArticleCallout } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleCallout'
import { MLAITemplateResourceCTA } from '../../../components/articles/MLAITemplateResourceCTA'
import { ArticleReferences } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleReferences'
import { ArticleDisclaimer } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleDisclaimer'

export const useCustomHeader = true

const TOPIC = "A practical guide to startup funding in Australia"
export const CATEGORY = "featured"
export const SLUG = "a-practical-guide-to-startup-funding-in-australia"
export const DATE_PUBLISHED = "2026-05-12"
export const DATE_MODIFIED = "2026-05-12"
export const DESCRIPTION = "Startup funding Australia options for early founders, including grants, loans, angel investment, crowdfunding and readiness steps."
const HERO_IMAGE = "https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Fhero-92b547a9-906e-451c-a528-646e94987e24.jpg?alt=media&token=f0c705aa-4f91-4fcb-b687-70c67bf63871"
const HERO_IMAGE_ALT = "Early founders reviewing startup funding options at a cafe table in Australia"
export const FEATURED_FOCUS = "funding"

const AUTHOR_PROFILE = getAuthorProfile(DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY)
const AUTHOR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.name ?? 'Dr Sam Donegan'
const AUTHOR_ROLE = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.role ?? AUTHOR_PROFILE?.credentials ?? 'Founder'
const AUTHOR_BIO = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.bio ?? ''
const AUTHOR_AVATAR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.avatarUrl ?? DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL

interface FAQ {
  id: number
  question: string
  answer: ReactNode
}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "What startup funding options are available in Australia?", answer: "Australian startups can consider government grants and programs, loans, rebates, subsidies, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and bootstrapping. The right option depends on stage, eligibility, evidence and the purpose of the funding." },
  { id: 2, question: "Is there one business start up grant in Australia?", answer: "No. Startup support is not a single national grant. It is a changing mix of federal, state, territory and industry-specific programs, so founders should check current grant finder tools and relevant state support pages." },
  { id: 3, question: "How much money will the government give me for starting a business?", answer: "There is no universal amount. Government support varies by program, location, eligibility, funding purpose and round timing, and may include grants, loans, rebates or subsidies." },
  { id: 4, question: "When should a startup seek angel investment or venture capital?", answer: "Angel investment or venture capital is usually more suitable when a startup can show a credible growth story, early traction, a clear business model and a strong use of funds. Founders should also be ready for dilution, scrutiny and governance expectations." },
  { id: 5, question: "What should founders prepare before applying for funding?", answer: "Founders should be able to explain the customer problem, solution, market, traction, team capability, business model, financial logic and intended use of funds. Grant applications should also answer the specific eligibility and assessment criteria." },
]

export const summaryHighlights = {
  heading: "Key facts: A practical guide to startup funding in Australia",
  intro: "Startup funding Australia options for early founders, including grants, loans, angel investment, crowdfunding and readiness steps.",
  items: [
    { label: "How do I get funding for my startup?", description: "Choose a funding path that fits your stage, such as a grant, loan, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding or bootstrapping. Then prepare evidence and apply through the relevant route." },
    { label: "How do I get funding for my startup idea?", description: "At idea stage, focus first on customer discovery, validation evidence and a clear next milestone. Small grants, bootstrapping or targeted support may fit better than a broad raise." },
    { label: "How do you get funding for a start-up?", description: "Start by matching the funding source to the business goal, traction and repayment or growth story. Funders usually need clear evidence of the problem, model, team and use of funds." },
  ],
}

export const articleMeta = {
  title: "A practical guide to startup funding in Australia",
  topic: TOPIC,
  category: CATEGORY,
  slug: SLUG,
  description: DESCRIPTION,
  datePublished: DATE_PUBLISHED,
  dateModified: DATE_MODIFIED,
  author: AUTHOR,
  image: HERO_IMAGE,
  imageAlt: HERO_IMAGE_ALT,
  featuredFocus: FEATURED_FOCUS,
}

const faqSchemaItems = [
  { question: "How do I get funding for my startup?", answer: "Choose a funding path that fits your stage, such as a grant, loan, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding or bootstrapping. Then prepare evidence and apply through the relevant route." },
  { question: "How do I get funding for my startup idea?", answer: "At idea stage, focus first on customer discovery, validation evidence and a clear next milestone. Small grants, bootstrapping or targeted support may fit better than a broad raise." },
  { question: "How do you get funding for a start-up?", answer: "Start by matching the funding source to the business goal, traction and repayment or growth story. Funders usually need clear evidence of the problem, model, team and use of funds." },
  { question: "What startup funding options are available in Australia?", answer: "Australian startups can consider government grants and programs, loans, rebates, subsidies, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and bootstrapping. The right option depends on stage, eligibility, evidence and the purpose of the funding." },
  { question: "Is there one business start up grant in Australia?", answer: "No. Startup support is not a single national grant. It is a changing mix of federal, state, territory and industry-specific programs, so founders should check current grant finder tools and relevant state support pages." },
  { question: "How much money will the government give me for starting a business?", answer: "There is no universal amount. Government support varies by program, location, eligibility, funding purpose and round timing, and may include grants, loans, rebates or subsidies." },
  { question: "When should a startup seek angel investment or venture capital?", answer: "Angel investment or venture capital is usually more suitable when a startup can show a credible growth story, early traction, a clear business model and a strong use of funds. Founders should also be ready for dilution, scrutiny and governance expectations." },
  { question: "What should founders prepare before applying for funding?", answer: "Founders should be able to explain the customer problem, solution, market, traction, team capability, business model, financial logic and intended use of funds. Grant applications should also answer the specific eligibility and assessment criteria." },
]

const faqStructuredData = faqSchemaItems.length
  ? JSON.stringify({
      '@context': 'https://schema.org',
      '@type': 'FAQPage',

        '@type': 'Question',
        name: item.question,
        acceptedAnswer: {
          '@type': 'Answer',
          text: item.answer,
        },
      })),
    })
  : null

payload;}\nfunction allComponents(){var nodes=componentNodes();var out=[];for(var i=0;i<nodes.length;i++){out.push(componentData(nodes[i],'component'));}return out;}\nfunction postMeasure(){post({type:'measure',components:allComponents()});}\nfunction box=el.getBoundingClientRect();var name=el.getAttribute('data-cf-component-label')||el.getAttribute('data-cf-component-id')||'component';var kind=el.getAttribute('data-cf-component-type')||'component';label.textContent=name+' ('+kind+')';label.style.left=Math.max(8,Math.min(box.left,window.innerWidth-260))+'px';label.style.top=Math.max(8,box.top-32)+'px';label.hidden=false;}\nfunction suppress(event){event.preventDefault();event.stopPropagation();if(event.stopImmediatePropagation)event.stopImmediatePropagation();}\ndocument.addEventListener('mouseover',function(event){ensureFallbackBoundaries();var target=event.target&&event.target.closest?event.target.closest('[data-cf-component-id]'):null;if(!target)return;if(active&&active!==target)active.classList.remove('cf-inspector-hover');active=target;target.classList.add('cf-inspector-hover');show(target);post(componentData(target,'hover'));},true);\ndocument.addEventListener('mouseout',function(event){if(!active)return;var message=event.data;if(!message||typeof message!=='object'||message.source!=='founder-tools-inspector')return;if(message.type==='setMode'){window.__cfArticleInspectorMode=message.mode==='inspect'?'inspect':'comment';post({type:'ready',mode:mode()});}else if(message.type==='setSelectedComponent'){setSelected(message.componentId||'');}});\npost({type:'ready',mode:mode()});\nsetTimeout(queueMeasure,0);\n})();"

function ContentFactoryInspectorBridge() {
  useEffect(() => {
    if (typeof window === 'undefined') return
    if (!new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).has('cfInspector')) return
    const script = document.createElement('script')
    script.dataset.contentFactoryInspector = 'true'
    script.textContent = CONTENT_FACTORY_INSPECTOR_SCRIPT
    document.body.appendChild(script)
    return () => {
      script.remove()
    }
  }, [])
  return null
}

export default function ArticleContent() {
  const authorDetails = {
    name: AUTHOR,
    role: AUTHOR_ROLE,
    bio: AUTHOR_BIO,
    avatarUrl: AUTHOR_AVATAR,
  }

  return (
    <>
      {faqStructuredData ? (
        <script type="application/ld+json" dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: faqStructuredData }} />
      ) : null}
      <ContentFactoryInspectorBridge />
      <ArticleHeroHeader
        breadcrumbs={[
          { label: 'Home', href: '/', icon: Home },
          { label: 'Articles', href: "/content" },
          { label: TOPIC, current: true },
        ]}
        title={TOPIC}
        titleHighlight={TOPIC}
        headerBgColor="cyan"
        summary={summaryHighlights}
        heroImage={HERO_IMAGE}
        heroImageAlt={HERO_IMAGE_ALT}
      />

      <ArticleTocPlaceholder className="bg-transparent" />

      <div className="prose prose-lg prose-slate max-w-none bg-transparent">
        <div id="intro" data-cf-component-id={"section:intro"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Why startup funding in Australia needs a staged plan"} data-cf-source-section-id={"intro"}>
        <p><strong>{TOPIC}</strong> — {"Startup funding in Australia is not one single pathway. Founders can look at government grants and support programs, loans, rebates, subsidies, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and other forms of capital. The useful question is not only \u201cwhere can we get money?\u201d It is \u201cwhich funding source fits our stage, evidence and goals?\u201d"}</p>
        <p>{"A grant or support program may help with a specific project, while investor capital usually needs a stronger story about growth, traction and the business model. Funding also sits inside a wider startup plan: customers, collaborators, talent, advisors and community support all affect whether capital can turn into real progress."}</p>
        <div data-cf-component-id={"image:intro"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Hero image"} data-cf-source-section-id={"intro"}>
        <ArticleImageBlock
          src={HERO_IMAGE}
          alt={HERO_IMAGE_ALT}
          caption="Startup funding Australia options for early founders, including grants, loans, angel investment, crowdfunding and readiness steps."
          width={1600}
          height={1067}
        />
        </div>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-type={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-label={"Who is this guide for?"}>
          <AudienceGrid
            heading="Who is this guide for?"
            cards={[
            {
              title: 'Founders & Builders',
              description: 'For operators validating demand, pitching a vision, and moving before momentum stalls.',
              icon: <RocketLaunchIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'orange',
            },
            {
              title: 'Students & Switchers',
              description: 'For readers learning how strong technical partners evaluate traction, skills, and fit.',
              icon: <AcademicCapIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'purple',
            },
            {
              title: 'Community Builders',
              description: 'For connectors, mentors, and organisers helping founders meet collaborators in the right rooms.',
              icon: <UsersIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'yellow',
            },
            ]}
          />
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:key-insight"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Key insight"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Key insight" variant="purple">
            {"Choose a funding path that fits your stage, such as a grant, loan, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding or bootstrapping. Then prepare evidence and apply through the relevant route."}
          </QuoteBlock>
        </div>
        <div id="funding-landscape" data-cf-component-id={"section:funding-landscape"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Start with the Australian funding landscape"} data-cf-source-section-id={"funding-landscape"}>
          <h2>{"Start with the Australian funding landscape"}</h2>
          <p>{"Startup funding in Australia is not one single market. It includes national, state and territory programs, plus local startup ecosystems that help founders find support, introductions and momentum. The NSW Small Business Commissioner notes that grants, loans, rebates and subsidies are available across a range of industries, business types and purposes, including startups."}</p>
          <p>{"The scale of that support also means founders need to filter carefully. The same NSW source says the Federal Government grant finder listed 537 different grants and programs for businesses, with NSW also offering its own grant finder. For a founder, this is useful but noisy. A better first step is to narrow by location, sector, business stage and the purpose of the funding."}</p>
          <p>{"State and city ecosystems matter because funding often comes through more than a form on a website. South Australia shows how local ecosystems can build startup capacity over time. The Department of State Development says South Australia\u2019s startup ecosystem has grown significantly over the last decade, and cites Startup Genome 2024 ranking Adelaide as a Top 5 city in Oceania for overall sector performance."}</p>
          <p>{"For Australian AI and machine learning founders, the practical lesson is to map both money and ecosystem access. Look at grants and programs, but also look at the networks around them: events, innovation hubs, startup support programs and local industry communities. These can help founders understand what support fits their stage before they spend time on applications or investor conversations."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:funding-landscape"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: Start with the Australian funding landscape"} data-cf-source-section-id={"funding-landscape"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-a24b0318-e326-42f0-b478-4e61aa588d80.jpg?alt=media&token=737c0811-1057-42ee-8ebd-9ea0a7563445"
            alt="Notebook and coffee on a startup desk with Australian funding notes and a blurred whiteboard in the background"
            caption="Start with the Australian funding landscape"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <ArticleCallout title="Funding starts with fit" variant="brand">
            {"Shortlist opportunities by state, sector, business stage and funding purpose before you spend time on applications or investor outreach."}
          </ArticleCallout>
        </div>
        <div id="capital-options" data-cf-component-id={"section:capital-options"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Match the funding option to your stage"} data-cf-source-section-id={"capital-options"}>
          <h2>{"Match the funding option to your stage"}</h2>
          <p>{"The best choice for startup funding in Australia depends on what you are trying to prove next. A grant, loan, angel cheque or crowdfunding campaign can all be useful in the right context."}</p>
          <p>{"Government-backed grants can help with eligible business activity, innovation projects, training, hiring or other defined purposes. They are worth checking at federal, state and territory level, but founders should expect competition and prepare a strong application. Conventional finance and business loans are different. They are usually better suited to businesses with clearer revenue, assets or repayment capacity, rather than a very early idea with uncertain cash flow."}</p>
          <p>{"Equity funding is usually a growth decision, not just a cash decision. Angel investors and venture capital can bring capital, networks and experience, but they also come with investor scrutiny, dilution and governance expectations. Equity crowdfunding can be another path for some startups, especially where the product and audience support a public campaign, but it needs to fit the company\u2019s compliance needs and ability to run the campaign well."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:capital-options"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: Match the funding option to your stage"} data-cf-source-section-id={"capital-options"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-4c0c69fc-8ad4-47d9-b7ca-8fcc93b6b51a.jpg?alt=media&token=9b1154a3-0e1a-498f-87b3-a449a75f50de"
            alt="Startup funding notes on a desk with grant forms and loan paperwork in a candid Australian workspace"
            caption="Match the funding option to your stage"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <h3>{"A simple stage-by-stage comparison"}</h3>
          <p>{"At the idea validation stage, founders should be careful about chasing capital that requires a polished growth story before the basics are clear. Small grants, bootstrapping and customer discovery work may be more realistic than loans or equity rounds. The aim is to test whether the problem, user and early offer are strong enough to justify more formal funding."}</p>
          <p>{"At the early traction stage, the funding question becomes more specific. If you have users, early revenue or a clearer product plan, grants may support defined projects and angel investors may be more willing to look at the opportunity. At scale preparation, founders need a stronger business foundation, a clear capital strategy and investor-ready materials before approaching larger equity investors or more structured finance options."}</p>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-type={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Get the checklist"}>
          <ArticleResourceCTA
            eyebrow="Free guide"
            title={"Get the startup funding australia checklist"}
            description="Use this article as a working guide: shortlist candidates, validate traction, and structure your next conversations."
            buttonLabel="Download now"
            buttonHref="/content"
            accent="purple"
          />
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"step-list:practical-next-steps"} data-cf-component-type={"step-list"} data-cf-component-label={"Practical next steps"}>
          <ArticleStepList
            title="Practical next steps"
            steps={[
            "Define the funding goal: decide what the money will help you do and which funding path fits that goal.",
            "Tailor the pitch or application: adjust the deck or grant response to the funder, the criteria and the stage of your startup.",
            ]}
            accent="indigo"
          />
        </div>
        <div id="investor-and-grant-readiness" data-cf-component-id={"section:investor-and-grant-readiness"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Build the evidence funders expect to see"} data-cf-source-section-id={"investor-and-grant-readiness"}>
          <h2>{"Build the evidence funders expect to see"}</h2>
          <p>{"Before you chase startup funding in Australia, make the business easier to assess. A strong foundation means you can explain the customer problem, the solution, the business model and the growth goal in plain terms. This matters across capital types. Investor pathways, grants, loans and other funding options all become harder when the founder cannot show why the business needs money now and how that money supports growth."}</p>
          <p>{"For investor funding, prepare a clear pitch deck that covers the problem, solution, market, traction, team, business model and intended use of funds. For grants, treat the application as a direct response to the program, not a generic company profile. Read the eligibility criteria closely, match your answers to the purpose of the grant, and show why your business fits the funding opportunity. Grant programs can be competitive, so clarity and relevance matter."}</p>
          <ul>
            <li>{"Define the funding goal: decide what the money will help you do and which funding path fits that goal."}</li>
            <li>{"Tailor the pitch or application: adjust the deck or grant response to the funder, the criteria and the stage of your startup."}</li>
          </ul>
        </div>
        <div id="common-founder-questions" data-cf-component-id={"section:common-founder-questions"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Answer the funding questions before you apply"} data-cf-source-section-id={"common-founder-questions"}>
          <h2>{"Answer the funding questions before you apply"}</h2>
          <p>{"Before you apply for startup funding in Australia, be clear on what kind of funding you are seeking and why it fits your stage. Australian founders can look at several paths, including bank finance, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and government-backed grants. The right choice depends on the business goal, the evidence you already have and the type of capital you can realistically use well."}</p>
          <p>{"If you are still at idea stage, the first funding question is not usually \u201chow much can I raise?\u201d It is \u201cwhat evidence will make this idea worth funding?\u201d Customer discovery, early validation and a focused next milestone can make a small funding request stronger than a broad raise with little proof. Investors and grant assessors both need a reason to believe the business can turn funding into progress."}</p>
          <p>{"Government support is not one fixed startup grant or one standard amount. Federal, state and territory programs change over time, and support can include grants, loans, rebates and subsidies for different industries, business types and purposes. Founders should check current grant finder tools and the relevant state or territory support pages before planning around any specific program."}</p>
          <p>{"The same logic applies if you have a defined budget, such as $100K, and are deciding what business to start. The better question is which model can turn that money into validated demand, measurable traction and a fundable next step. A capital plan should show how the money moves the startup from its current stage to the next credible milestone, not just how much cash the founder hopes to receive."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:common-founder-questions"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: Answer the funding questions before you apply"} data-cf-source-section-id={"common-founder-questions"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-26df8e80-72a5-4f91-9543-6aa8f5a1607e.jpg?alt=media&token=94cdd841-511d-4a43-aa00-7da92ec301be"
            alt="Founder\u2019s hand circling startup funding options in a notebook beside a laptop on a cluttered desk"
            caption="Answer the funding questions before you apply"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
        </div>
        <div id="conclusion" data-cf-component-id={"section:conclusion"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Choose your next funding move"} data-cf-source-section-id={"conclusion"}>
          <h2>{"Choose your next funding move"}</h2>
          <p>{"The best next step is not to chase every startup funding Australia option at once. Shortlist two or three routes that fit your current stage, such as grants, angel investment, venture capital, bank finance, equity crowdfunding or bootstrapping. Then check the timing, eligibility and effort required before you commit time to an application or raise."}</p>
          <p>{"Use the next week to tighten the evidence behind your funding story. Make sure you can explain the customer problem, your early traction, the team\u2019s capability, the financial logic and how the funds will be used. Grants are competitive, so a stronger application matters. Investors also expect a clear business foundation and a funding strategy that matches your goals."}</p>
          <p>{"Australian startup ecosystems are built around events, networks, startup supports and founder communities. For AI and machine learning founders, MLAI can help turn funding preparation into practical momentum through learning, founder tools and conversations with other Australian builders."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:conclusion"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: Choose your next funding move"} data-cf-source-section-id={"conclusion"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-3ff72cbe-8a47-46d5-931d-efceb41d7c85.jpg?alt=media&token=222e5937-51cd-4aaf-8315-969a0923e0b6"
            alt="Startup founders comparing funding options in a candid wide office meeting scene"
            caption="Choose your next funding move"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:keep-moving-forward"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Keep moving forward"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Keep moving forward" variant="orange">
            {"Start by matching the funding source to the business goal, traction and repayment or growth story. Funders usually need clear evidence of the problem, model, team and use of funds."}
          </QuoteBlock>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"template-resource-cta"} data-cf-component-type={"template-resource-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Free MLAI Template Resource"}>
          <MLAITemplateResourceCTA />
        </div>

      <div data-cf-component-id={"references"} data-cf-component-type={"references"} data-cf-component-label={"Authoritative References"}>
        <ArticleReferences
          references={[
            {id: 1, href: "https://statedevelopment.sa.gov.au/startup-ecosystem", title: "South Australia\u2019s startup ecosystem | Department of State Development", publisher: "statedevelopment.sa.gov.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting South Australia\u2019s startup ecosystem | Department of State Development.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 2, href: "https://www.bentleys.com.au/resources/startup-funding-strategy-advising-australian-entrepreneurs-on-how-to-get-capital-growth/", title: "Startup Funding Australia: Get Capital & Grow Your Business", publisher: "bentleys.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Startup Funding Australia: Get Capital & Grow Your Business.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 3, href: "https://business.gov.au/grants-and-programs/industry-growth-program", title: "Industry Growth Program | business.gov.au", publisher: "business.gov.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Industry Growth Program | business.gov.au.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 4, href: "https://www.editiongroup.com/au/insights/startup-grants-australia", title: "Startup Grants for Australian Tech Companies - Free Guide", publisher: "editiongroup.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Startup Grants for Australian Tech Companies - Free Guide.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 5, href: "https://www.smallbusiness.nsw.gov.au/news-podcasts/news/government-grants-and-support-for-small-business-0", title: "Government grants and support for small business | NSW Small Business Commissioner", publisher: "smallbusiness.nsw.gov.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Government grants and support for small business | NSW Small Business Commissioner.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 6, href: "https://www.merge.com.au/the-australian-startup-seed-funding-guide/", title: "The Australian Startup Seed Funding Guide - Merge Marketing | Hospitality & Restaurant Marketing Agency", publisher: "merge.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting The Australian Startup Seed Funding Guide - Merge Marketing | Hospitality & Restaurant Marketing Agency.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 7, href: "https://fundingguide.com.au/blogs/news/how-to-find-investors-for-your-startup-in-australia?srsltid=AfmBOorna5VGLC2NmtQEJNpNxs10A8mX04eiXfYkv1KdJnWEFha7CUdA", title: "How to Find Investors for Your Startup in Australia \u2013 The Funding Guide", publisher: "fundingguide.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting How to Find Investors for Your Startup in Australia \u2013 The Funding Guide.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 8, href: "https://bluesaltconsulting.com.au/grants-for-businesses/", title: "Grant Series: Grants for Business - BlueSalt", publisher: "bluesaltconsulting.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Grant Series: Grants for Business - BlueSalt.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 9, href: "https://squareup.com/au/en/business-launchpad/grow/how-to-access-government-grants", title: "How to Access Australian Small Business Grants | Square", publisher: "squareup.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting How to Access Australian Small Business Grants | Square.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 10, href: "https://gocardless.com/en-au/guides/posts/guide-startup-business-funding-australia/", title: "A Guide to Start-Up Business Funding in Australia | GoCardless", publisher: "gocardless.com", description: "Authoritative reference supporting A Guide to Start-Up Business Funding in Australia | GoCardless.", category: "guide"},
            {id: 11, href: "https://www.smartcompany.com.au/startupsmart/five-aussie-startups-raised-95-45-million-this-week/", title: "Five Aussie startups that raised $95.45 million this week", publisher: "smartcompany.com.au", description: "Authoritative reference supporting Five Aussie startups that raised $95.45 million this week.", category: "guide"},
          ]}
          heading="Sources & further reading"
        />
      </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"disclaimer"} data-cf-component-type={"disclaimer"} data-cf-component-label={"Disclaimer"}>
          <ArticleDisclaimer />
        </div>

        <div className="my-12 not-prose" data-cf-component-id={"cta"} data-cf-component-type={"company-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Company CTA"}>
          <ArticleCompanyCTA

            body="Use MLAI to connect funding preparation with practical learning, founder tools and conversations with other Australian AI and machine learning builders."
            buttonText="Explore MLAI founder support"
            buttonHref="/ai-founder-support-pitching-and-investor-readiness"
          />
        </div>
      </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"author-bio"} data-cf-component-type={"author-bio"} data-cf-component-label={"About the Author"}>

        </div>

        <div className="mt-12" data-cf-component-id={"faq"} data-cf-component-type={"faq"} data-cf-component-label={"FAQ"}>
          <ArticleFAQ items={faqItems} />
        </div>

        <ArticleFooterNav backHref="/content" topHref="#" />
    </>
  )
}

import { useEffect } from 'react'
import type { ReactNode } from 'react'
import { Home } from 'lucide-react'
import { RocketLaunchIcon, AcademicCapIcon, UsersIcon } from '@heroicons/react/24/outline'
import { DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY, getAuthorProfile, DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL } from '../../authors'
import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ'
import ArticleCompanyCTA from '../../../components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA'
import AuthorBio from '../../../components/AuthorBio'

interface FAQ {
  id: number
  question: string

}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "What startup funding options are available in Australia?", answer: "Australian startups can consider government grants and programs, loans, rebates, subsidies, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and bootstrapping. The right option depends on stage, eligibility, evidence and the purpose of the funding." },
  { id: 2, question: "Is there one business start up grant in Australia?", answer: "No. Startup support is not a single national grant. It is a changing mix of federal, state, territory and industry-specific programs, so founders should check current grant finder tools and relevant state support pages." },
  { id: 3, question: "How much money will the government give me for starting a business?", answer: "There is no universal amount. Government support varies by program, location, eligibility, funding purpose and round timing, and may include grants, loans, rebates or subsidies." },
  { id: 4, question: "When should a startup seek angel investment or venture capital?", answer: "Angel investment or venture capital is usually more suitable when a startup can show a credible growth story, early traction, a clear business model and a strong use of funds. Founders should also be ready for dilution, scrutiny and governance expectations." },
  { id: 5, question: "What should founders prepare before applying for funding?", answer: "Founders should be able to explain the customer problem, solution, market, traction, team capability, business model, financial logic and intended use of funds. Grant applications should also answer the specific eligibility and assessment criteria." },
]

  ],
}

function ContentFactoryInspectorBridge() {
  useEffect(() => {
    if (typeof window === 'undefined') return
    if (!new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).has('cfInspector')) return
    const script = document.createElement('script')
    script.dataset.contentFactoryInspector = 'true'
    script.textContent = CONTENT_FACTORY_INSPECTOR_SCRIPT
    document.body.appendChild(script)
    return () => {
      script.remove()
    }
  }, [])
  return null
}

        <div id="intro" data-cf-component-id={"section:intro"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Why startup funding in Australia needs a staged plan"} data-cf-source-section-id={"intro"}>
        <p><strong>{TOPIC}</strong> — {"Startup funding in Australia is not one single pathway. Founders can look at government grants and support programs, loans, rebates, subsidies, angel investment, venture capital, equity crowdfunding and other forms of capital. The useful question is not only \u201cwhere can we get money?\u201d It is \u201cwhich funding source fits our stage, evidence and goals?\u201d"}</p>
        <p>{"A grant or support program may help with a specific project, while investor capital usually needs a stronger story about growth, traction and the business model.         <div data-cf-component-id={"image:intro"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Hero image"} data-cf-source-section-id={"intro"}>
        <ArticleImageBlock
          src={HERO_IMAGE}

          width={1600}
          height={1067}
        />
        </div>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-type={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-label={"Who is this guide for?"}>
          <AudienceGrid
            heading="Who is this guide for?"
            cards={[
            {
              title: 'Founders & Builders',
              description: 'For operators validating demand, pitching a vision, and moving before momentum stalls.',
              icon: <RocketLaunchIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'orange',
            },
            {
              title: 'Students & Switchers',
              description: 'For readers learning how strong technical partners evaluate traction, skills, and fit.',
              icon: <AcademicCapIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'purple',
            },
            {
              title: 'Community Builders',
              description: 'For connectors, mentors, and organisers helping founders meet collaborators in the right rooms.',
              icon: <UsersIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
